features.summary: NVIDIA reported record revenue of $8.3 billion for Q1 FY2023, up 8% sequentially and 46% year-on-year, driven by strong performance in Data Center and Gaming segments. Data Center revenue reached $3.8 billion, growing 15% sequentially and 83% year-on-year, while Gaming revenue was $3.6 billion, up 6% sequentially and 31% year-on-year. The company anticipates a sequential decline in Gaming revenue for Q2 due to macroeconomic challenges, particularly in Europe and China.
features.management_outlook: For Q2 FY2023, NVIDIA expects revenue of approximately $8.1 billion, plus or minus 2%, with anticipated impacts of $500 million from the Russia-Ukraine conflict and COVID lockdowns in China. The company projects strong growth in Data Center and Automotive segments, offset by a decline in Gaming. Gross margins are expected to remain stable, with GAAP gross margin at 65.1% and non-GAAP gross margin at 67.1%.
features.risks: NVIDIA faces significant risks from geopolitical tensions, particularly the ongoing war in Ukraine and COVID-related lockdowns in China, which are impacting both supply chains and demand. The company has noted softness in parts of Europe and China, which could affect future revenue. Additionally, the potential decline in cryptocurrency mining demand may further impact the Gaming segment.
features.key_drivers: Key growth drivers for NVIDIA include the strong adoption of its A100 GPU in Data Centers, the successful RTX 30 Series in Gaming, and the ramp-up of the Orin robotics processor in the Automotive sector. The company is also focusing on expanding its AI capabilities through new architectures like Hopper and Grace, as well as enhancing its software platforms such as NVIDIA AI and Omniverse, which are expected to drive future growth.
